perturbation.original_text: The Impacted Party shall give notice within seven (7) days of the Force Majeure Event to the other party, stating the period of time the occurrence is expected to continue.
perturbation.changed_text: The Impacted Party shall give notice within one (1) day of the Force Majeure Event to the other party, stating the period of time the occurrence is expected to continue.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notice period for a Force Majeure Event to one day may not provide sufficient time for a company to assess the situation and provide an accurate estimate, conflicting with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contracts, especially in New York.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY UCC § 1-304 states that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.'  Reducing the notification period to one day could be seen as acting in bad faith if it hinders the ability to properly assess and report the impact of the event.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 1-304 Obligation of Good Faith Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 1, PART 3 Section 1--304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notification period for a Force Majeure event from seven days to one day. The Perturbation Explanation states that this change might not provide sufficient time to assess the situation properly, potentially conflicting with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. The Law Explanation specifies that NY UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in contract performance. Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly quotes NY UCC § 1-304, stating, 'Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By significantly shortening the notification period, the changed text creates a risk that the impacted party will be unable to fulfill its obligation to act in good faith, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time if any of the following occur: (i) the other party fails to comply with any requirements or obligations under this Agreement, and such non-compliance is not cured within 30 days following written notice from the other party identifying the non-compliance;
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time if any of the following occur: (i) the other party fails to comply with any requirements or obligations under this Agreement, and such non-compliance is not cured within 10 days following written notice from the other party identifying the non-compliance;
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period to 10 days might be considered unreasonable and not in accordance with good faith business practices, as it might not allow sufficient time to rectify complex non-compliance issues. This is especially pertinent given that the contract is governed by New York law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Reducing the cure period to 10 days could be viewed as a breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under New York law (NY UCC § 1-304), because it significantly limits the breaching party's ability to remedy the non-compliance, potentially leading to unjust termination.

Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the cure period from 30 days to 10 days, which is explained as potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under New York law. The Law Explanation suggests that this significantly limits the breaching party's ability to remedy the non-compliance, potentially leading to unjust termination. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically NY UCC § 1-304, states that 'Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By shortening the cure period so drastically, the modified contract may not allow sufficient time to rectify complex non-compliance issues, thus potentially contradicting the obligation of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and any dispute arising hereunder shall be determined in accordance with, the laws of State of New York (without giving effect to conflict of laws principles) including all matters of construction, validity and performance.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and any dispute arising hereunder shall be determined in accordance with, the laws of the People's Republic of China (without giving effect to conflict of laws principles) including all matters of construction, validity and performance.
perturbation.explanation: Switching the governing law from New York to the People's Republic of China may violate public policy if the application of Chinese law would lead to outcomes that are fundamentally unfair or contrary to established legal principles in the United States, particularly concerning intellectual property rights and trade secret protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Public Policy Exception to Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 allows a court to refuse to apply the law of the chosen state if it is contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue. Enforcing intellectual property rights is a key consideration in US public policy. The rights of the owner of intellectual property may be reduced if China's IP laws are enforced.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the governing law from New York to the People's Republic of China. The Law Explanation argues that this could reduce intellectual property rights, conflicting with US public policy. The Perturbation Explanation suggests a potential violation of public policy if Chinese law leads to unfair outcomes, particularly concerning intellectual property and trade secret protection. Although the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the consistency between the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation supports the existence of a legal contradiction.
